author: @michellejaeandrews4799
text:
This discussion is so interesting.  I work at a heritage museum giving education to 5 to 7-year-old school children.  I try to convey how clever our ancestors were despite not having any electricity.  I attempt to explain the principles of evaporative cooling for keeping food cool in summer and why the whole wood stove is hot through conductive heating even though you light a fire in just one small place.  I also like to explain how our butter churn works with gears.  In this way, I hope I am helping to prepare them for a future of reduced availability of electrical energy.  We can learn so very much by looking to the past!  

The other day, when I was showing the kids how to do hand washing using a washboard, I asked them how their Mummy or Daddy operated their washing machine at home.  One little fella answered that they put the clothes into the machine and speak to it to set the wash cycle!!  Amazing!  But lazy, and I think it reduces a person's capabilities.  That's why I love this channel so much - thank you Robert for giving us some agency and understanding.

author: @fredhair
text:
Totally agree with this. Here in the UK it can get pretty cold, but unless you're in Scotland it's not that bad. Young healthy people in my view; should have a good enough metabolism that they don't need the heating on at any point, they only really need to wrap themselves in a couple more layers. If you feed yourself, your body will do a very good job of keeping you warm. If you do need some outside help to get you warmed up, don't heat your whole house just to warm your toes when you could wrap in a blanket with a hot water bottle or something else.
As others have mentioned, you want to reduce damp in the house though so it can be worth heating the whole house from time to time or at least heating rooms with poor ventilation to get the moisture into the hot air and flowing out of the house. I wake up at 6am and turn on a small oil filled radiator for an hour (within my off-peak economy 7 hours) just to keep the chill off and the damp at bay but other than that I use a hot water bottle in bed as I have a hot drink in the evening anyway, and generally wrap up warm as needed, eat more & fatty foods as well as going for a brisk walk early morning and having a cool shower + breathing exercise to stay warm and keep the blood circulating well.

author: @MindfulnessArtCoaching
text:
We are renting an old house. Our landlord insulated it in September. It previously had no insulation. We tried just heating ourselves, however this caused a lot of problems. We had damp walls which caused black mould to develop. We have now put the heating back on. This go rid of the damp walls. We still have high relative humidity. 

I think we shouldn't loose sight of the damage that high RH and mould can do to a property. It's great all this advice about saving money but when it can cost you £20,000 and upwards to fix problems caused by damp. These shouldn't be ignored. It's not being talked about.

Yes it's costing us about £5.40 a day to heat our house at the moment. This adds up to considerably less than what it would if we were to let mould and damp damage the house.

author: @Xero1of1
text:
Hmm. My house is roughly 1800sqft of livable space, not including the basement. I spent most of my time in either the office (I work from home most days) or my bedroom. So, maybe 1/12 of the total space. I generally like it warm, so I keep my house at 73°F in the winter, 75° in the summer, and I live in Wisconsin. I have central heating/cooling with natural gas for heating. Electricity costs are $0.13724/kwh.  Gas is $0.33/therm, and I guess a therm is 100K BTUs. I have two electric space heaters already, one in the office and the other in the bedroom... and I wonder if it would be cheaper to lower the house temp to 68° or even 65° and just use the space heaters to heat just the rooms I'm in. On my last bill for October, heating was 20% of the bill with the average outside temp being 53°F. 
When it's running, the space heater uses just under 900W, so it costs about $0.12 per hour that it runs. It'll run at intervals based on room temp, so if I run it for the 16 or so hours that I'm in the office per day, and the 8 hours in the bedroom for sleeping, that'd cost $0.72 per day assuming a 25/75 on/off time. Roughly $22/mo just to run the space heaters. I don't know if reducing the house temp will provide the $22 in recouped costs from the space heaters, but we'll see how it goes. Gas is pretty cheap here.

It looks like the average electricity cost in the UK will be 34p($0.40)/kwh and September looks like gas was 234p($2.8)/therm. So your electricity is just about 3x more expensive and natural gas is 8.5x more expensive than here in Wisconsin. If I were to use your costs and compare it to mine, my last bill would have been $602.64 vs $248.31... yikes... over the course of this winter, I'd pay an additional $1,400 assuming the same usage and even more if the temperature stays below freezing. I can understand why you've been on your heating alternatives tangent now, lol.

text: The Neucleation of water condensation on to cold surfaces is the root cause of mould growth and massive heat loss. I have an east facing small bedroom, which never sees solar gain, so it does get cold in the winter  and is a target for mould.  I use a dehumidifier in there all the time, it keeps the moisture down and puts warmth into the room, it's amazing how much water I empty out of it every couple of days.

author: @davidcole5803

author: @JohnDoe-cf8jz
text: I live in a small rural village and at 59 with no work available. I dont get benefits such a universal credit or disability (even though I qualify if I could get to a doctor), although I do get help from the council for rent. I'm living on scant savings rapidly depleting and can't afford heating as I see it until it's a life or death situation. As of yet I haven't turned on any heating this year and it now hitting about 10-11 degrees in the one room I stay in. The little terraced council cottage is well insulated but I know that the serious cold is coming soon and I'm desperately thinking what to do. The first thing I've implemented is only using one room (living room) as mentioned earlier and I keep the doors closed to the little hall, kitchen and "supposed to be" bedroom. I wear a woolly hat, two shirts, thermal leggings and two pairs of socks all the time. I bought the cheapest electric "throw" blanket I could as I heard they are quite efficient, but haven't tried it yet, saving that until really cold. I hope things will get better but I fear the worst from all the news going on. Just hoping I don't get pneumonia, but at least that would be quick.

author: @mfbfreak
text:
The annoying bit is that if you keep your whole house at a much lower temperature, while only heating smaller parts of it to a comfy temperature, is that you can get severe humidity problems. With a lower temperature, the relative humidity goes up. 
I have some double (but not HR++) glazing in my bedroom, in wooden window frames. If i completely turn off the central heating on a cold winter night, there will be enough condensation on the windows to soak the window sills, causing rot and fungus on the long term. 
The ventilation's always open.
Any ideas about how to avoid those kinds of problems? If i just keep my bedroom at at least 18 degrees at night (heater will turn on once, maybe twice during the night), it's not much of a problem, but if heating gets so expensive that my family can't afford running the central heating on a low setting at night anymore, it would be a pretty serious problem.

replies.author: @ShivaTD420
replies.text:
Usually a clear plastic film taped air tight to windows. This will prevent the moisure from condensing on the cold window.

Generally rh drops with temp. In homes warm air holds the humidity produced by breathing, cooking, showers. If this air can reach a cold surface the air drops the humidity it was holding on the surface. This is usually why good vapor barriers are needed in cold climates.

I gather when your place is held at a hotter temp the window is kept warm enough that moisure doesn't drop out.

author: @scotttovey
text:
I seen a documentary some years ago that tracked the migration of people from Asia to the Americas where they became American Indian tribes.

The crew visited a goat herder in Siberia in the dead of winter.
The shelter consisted of a large tent made from animal skins.

Within that large tent were smaller tents that were used for sleeping.

Within that sleeping tent was a single candle that was lit and burned throughout the night to keep the occupant warm.

The journalist doing the story said he was so warm, that he had to take his winter gear off in order to be comfortable.

The tent within a tent creates multiple micro climates.

author: @MontanaEthicalHackers
text: Six years ago, I was programming from a warehouse.  I can attest that few of these methods work at a certain point if your insulation is very poor.  I stayed there for one winter.  Holding hot water or keeping a fire under your toes isn't enough to negate the fierce distraction of the cold.  I've since upgraded to a forced air 5,100 sqft office building downtown staying at a toasty 75F(24C) with $350 winter heating bills in a 30F(-1C) January average climate.   My ceiling has blow-in insulation 2ft deep(60cm) and my walls are 2ft of stone.  I only have 1000sqft(93m2) of external facing walls as my building is sandwiched between two others.  Stone is excellent and I wish more houses in America were made of stone instead of brick.  Good architecture, planning, materials, and maintenance can preposition a society to be resilient towards heating challenges.  My building was designed and built in 1890.  Our ancestors weren't in any way stupid.  America should stop building houses out of wood and focus on building houses that our great grand children will benefit from.  Stone, concrete, and steel is the way to go.

author: @upnorthandpersonal
text: The key to staying warm is thermal mass. I heat my off-grid place here in Finland to 22C with barely any effort. In the north, people in the old days used masonry fireplaces to keep their houses warm. A large mass that you heat up as efficiently as possible by burning wood at high temperature and storing the generated heat in the mass, which then gradually releases it to the building. The rocket mass heater got it's idea from this. Today, I use two systems: 1) a traditional masonry fireplace and 2) radiant floor heating powered by a wood gasification burner coupled to a 3000L water tank. I use around 10m^3 of wood a year, all from my own forest, processed (cut/split) in summer with electric tools powered by my solar panels - essentially storing solar energy in summer for winter use :)

author: @ProlificInvention
text: I have many heating solutions and inventions I have been working on for years, the one I worked on and actually used in an emergency furnace outage last year (during an ice storm) Is based on those Chinese Diesel Heaters.  If you're not familiar they are a type of mini forced air heater utilizing a heat exchanger and are exceedingly efficient and durable, originally designed to heat semi truck cabs.  They are very popular amongst RVers, off grid, van lifers, and tinkerer's worldwide due to their small size, modularity, efficiency, and simplicity.  I Installed 2 of them on a rolling cart with a 100ah battery, a 2000 watt inverter, a 0-48v Variable AC/DC converter, a small 1500 watt generator, and a 15amp battery charger.  I also have insulted ducting and a window insert for bringing in hot air as well as recirculating indoor air to be heated.  Those 2 heaters kept my entire 2000 sq foot house above 70 degrees °F for 2 days until my furnace guy got there to replace the blower fan.   I also heated my garage and Chevrolet Tahoe with their own diesel heaters, In the garage I used an old cast iron steam radiator to scavenge over 10,000btus of heat that most people just duct into the environment and it had many benefits and zero drawbacks-I even cooked and heated water on the hot iron radiator which reached temps 0f 400°F near the exhaust input side, but the output exhaust was only 90°f and had no diesel smell which is another benefit.  As far as heating my Tahoe, I did that during heavy snowstorms and ice storms so I didn't have to scrape snow off both at home and at work, it was also nice to always have a warm vehicle and it only costed on average $1.50 a day in fuel when used.  Anyway, awesome videos Robert, love your channel.

author: @cxzuk
text:
You made a comment on a recent video about not seeing the value in the internet of things and smart devices - And others have also already mentioned zone heating which is akin to a micro-climate.

This isn't exactly in your channels spirit so didn't mention before, but I've a smart heating system (drayton wiser controller and radiator thermostats). Its benefits are the radiators can communicate with the boiler and the other radiators, and collaboratively they can create zoned heating as well as the normal smart stuff, like scheduling, open window detection etc. From my experience and others online, this can save 10-15% on bills from adding some smarts to it. 

The big picture idea is some smarts in our systems can hopefully remove some of inefficiencies

author: @stupidscruff
text:
I've got a tent I'm thinking of throwing up in the front room if it gets that bad lol nice little micro climate there.
Another thing to bear in mind is modern houses are much better insulated then older.
My partners old house was freezing cold in the winter, I warmed it significantly by resealing all the windows and doors, throwing some cheap insulation down in the attic and fitting draught excluders.
I worked out the cold air flow was coming through the front windows and porch, rubbers in the front door frames worked a treat then finally rearranging the furniture so the radiators were more effective.
Good curtains made a big difference too.
She couldn't believe how much better it was and it all took me a day!

replies.author: @alistairmurray626
replies.text: ​@10thAveFreezeOut I was initially given one as a gift, it was marketed as a heat massager thing, that you put around your neck area, and it heats/repairs your muscles too. I have since found that you can simply make them out of an old pillow material filled with store bought rice, the microwave is also really efficient compared to cookers or kettles, much cheaper to heat up. I put mine on top of my slippers (to heat them for later, plus insulated against the ground), then put new socks on top (for later, and because it's too hot for my bare feet against the heating pad), then i put my bare feet on top of the socks and cover them with a towel, warms me right up for a few hours. plus when you have to get up you get the warm socks and slipper feeling that remains for another hour or so.

text: What about a decent hybrid thermal solar panel with a PV panel (at the base of it) to power the fan and for pumping the air into a thermal storage (and then to where u need it)?
